de: The Black Death was a severe outbreak of disease in England from 1348 to 1350.
en: The Black Death was a severe outbreak of bubonic plague in England from 1348 to 1350.

de: Explain the real cause of the Black Death.
en: The real cause was bubonic plague, spread by infected fleas that lived on rats.

de: The Black Death first reached England at ___.
en: The Black Death first reached England at Melcombe Regis in Dorset in June 1348.

de: Why did ports help the plague spread?
en: Ports helped the plague spread because infected rats and fleas travelled on ships.

de: One reason the plague spread quickly was ___.
en: One reason the plague spread quickly was that trade and shipping linked many towns and ports.

de: Early symptoms included sweating and coughing.
en: Early symptoms of the Black Death included sweating, coughing and fever.

de: What were buboes?
en: Buboes were large painful lumps that appeared on victims’ bodies, often on the arms and legs.

de: Why did some lumps turn black?
en: Some lumps turned black because blood dried out inside the body.

de: Most victims died within ___.
en: Most people who caught the Black Death died within five days.

de: Medieval people often blamed bad air.
en: Many medieval people believed the plague was caused by bad air, also called miasma.

de: Explain the religious explanation for the plague.
en: Many people believed the Black Death was a punishment from God.

de: Why did some people pray more during the plague?
en: Some people prayed more because they believed God could save them from the disease.

de: Why were doctors unable to cure the Black Death?
en: Doctors were unable to cure the Black Death because they did not understand bacteria, fleas or infection.

de: Name one attempted treatment.
en: Some people used dried flowers, herbs, toads, leeches, figs or butter as attempted treatments.

de: The Black Death killed about one-third of England’s population.
en: The Black Death killed about one-third of England’s population, around 1.5 million people.

de: How did the Black Death affect villages?
en: Many villages were abandoned because so many people died or moved away.

de: Why were some Jews attacked?
en: Some Jews were wrongly blamed for causing the disease, so they were attacked or forced into separate areas.

de: How did the Black Death affect farming?
en: With fewer peasants, agricultural land was unused and livestock were left to die.

de: Why did food prices rise?
en: Food prices rose because fewer workers meant lower food supplies.

de: Why could workers ask for higher wages?
en: Workers could demand higher wages because there were fewer peasants and craftsmen available.

de: How did lords respond to labour shortages?
en: Lords offered villeins more food and money to work their land.

de: What was the Ordinance of Labourers?
en: The Ordinance of Labourers was a 1349 law that tried to keep wages at pre-plague levels.

de: Why did many people lose faith in the Church?
en: Many people lost faith because priests, monks and those who prayed also died.

de: What happened in the next 100 years?
en: The plague returned roughly every 10 years and England’s population declined over the following century.

de: How was the Black Death linked to the Peasants’ Revolt?
en: The Black Death helped increase tensions over wages, labour duties and taxes before the Peasants’ Revolt of 1381.

de: Why is the Black Death historically significant?
en: It changed population, wages, villages, religious attitudes and the relationship between peasants and lords.

de: A common misconception is that rats alone caused the plague.
en: A better explanation is that infected fleas living on rats spread the bubonic plague.

de: What did the plague reveal about medieval medicine?
en: It showed that medieval medicine was based more on beliefs and observation than scientific understanding.
